title: Wallet / Ledger — System Design
description: Design a money ledger and wallet: hold balances, move funds between accounts, and never lose or invent a cent. The theme is correctness over availability — double-entry accounting, idempotency, concurrency control on balances, and reconciliation.

nodes.label: Client / Service
nodes.description: Initiates transfers, top-ups, and balance reads.

nodes.label: Wallet API
nodes.description: Validates requests, enforces idempotency, orchestrates ledger writes.

nodes.label: Ledger Service
nodes.description: Owns double-entry posting rules and transactional integrity.

nodes.label: Ledger Store
nodes.description: Authoritative ledger database (per-account shard). Holds the append-only journal plus the balance, idempotency, and outbox tables, so all write-path rows commit in one local transaction.

nodes.label: Balance Table
nodes.description: Durable, materialized per-account balance table in the ledger database, updated in the same transaction as the postings and rebuildable from the journal.

nodes.label: Idempotency Table
nodes.description: Durable table (in the ledger database) mapping request key + request hash -> result, committed in the posting transaction so retries don't double-post.

nodes.label: Outbox Table + Relay
nodes.description: Durable outbox table written in the posting transaction; a relay tails it and streams events to consumers at-least-once.

nodes.label: Reconciliation Worker
nodes.description: Recomputes balances from the journal and checks invariants.

nodes.label: Audit / Statement Store
nodes.description: Immutable export of postings for compliance and statements.

nodes.label: External Rail / PSP
nodes.description: Bank/PSP for funding and withdrawals outside the wallet; settles asynchronously and can accept, delay, fail, or reverse.

nodes.label: Settlement Tracker
nodes.description: Durable state machine for external movements (pending_external -> settled / failed / reversed), reconciled against provider reports.

nodes.label: Transfer Coordinator
nodes.description: Drives a cross-shard transfer saga from a durable cross_shard_transfers table: posts each shard-local leg through a per-shard clearing account, resumes idempotently after a crash, and compensates if a leg fails.

nodes.label: Ledger Shard A (source)
nodes.description: Source account's ledger shard. Leg 1 debits the source account and credits shard A's clearing account in one local transaction.

nodes.label: Ledger Shard B (destination)
nodes.description: Destination account's ledger shard. Leg 2 debits shard B's clearing account and credits the destination account in one local transaction.

functional: Transfer funds between two accounts atomically.
Top up (fund) from and withdraw to an external rail.
Read current balance and transaction history.
Every movement is recorded immutably and is auditable.
nonFunctional: Correctness above all: money is never created, lost, or double-counted.
Idempotent writes: a retried request must not move money twice.
Auditable: a complete, immutable history that always explains a balance.
Read-heavy on balances; writes must be strongly consistent and durable.

capacity.label: Logical transactions per second
capacity.note: Each is one balance-changing money movement.

capacity.label: Writes per transfer
capacity.note: 2 journal entries + 2 balance updates + 1 idempotency row + 1 outbox row (before index writes).

capacity.label: Durable write QPS
capacity.note: 10k txns x ~6 rows; with secondary indexes and replication the physical write rate is several times higher.

capacity.label: Journal growth
capacity.note: ~20k journal rows/s x ~31.5M s/yr; at ~100 B/row that is tens of TB/yr before indexes and replicas — retained indefinitely.

capacity.label: Balance reads
capacity.note: Dashboards and checks dominate; served O(1) from the materialized balance table.

capacity.label: Accounts
capacity.note: Sharded by account id; partition the journal by account shard and the statement index by (account_id, entry_id).

capacity.label: Hot-account limit
capacity.note: One very active account serializes on its balance row — bounded separately from aggregate TPS; mitigate with sub-account buckets that net.

capacity.label: History retention
capacity.note: Regulatory; never delete postings. Time/shard partitioning keeps old partitions cold.

api.description: Move funds from one account to another. Idempotent on the provided key. The decimal-string amount ("10.00") is canonicalized to integer minor units (1000) before validation and request-hashing, so equivalent representations ("10", "10.0", "10.00") hash identically. Errors: 409 idempotency_key_reused_with_different_request (key reused with a different payload), 422 insufficient_funds, 422 unsupported_currency, 423 account_frozen, 404 account_not_found.
Fund a wallet from an external rail. Idempotent on the key. Returns a pending movement; the balance is credited only when the rail settles.

Withdraw to an external rail. Places a hold against available_balance (not a posted debit), then captures the hold on a settled callback or releases it on failure. Returns the hold id. Errors: 422 insufficient_funds (against available_balance), 423 account_frozen, 422 unsupported_currency.

Provider webhook / settlement-event ingestion. Advances a movement to settled (capture the hold, post the debit), failed (release the hold), or reversed (compensating entry). Signature-verified and idempotent on a UNIQUE (provider, provider_event_id): a re-sent callback is processed exactly once. Error: 409 provider_event_already_seen.
Read the current balance (from the materialized balance table).
List postings affecting an account (statement). Paginated with a stable cursor and deterministic order (entry_id).

dataModel.name: transactions
dataModel.note: Transaction header: one row per logical money movement, grouping its balanced entries. Carries status, provenance, and metadata so any movement is explainable.

fields.name: type
fields.type: enum(transfer, topup, withdrawal, fee, reversal, adjustment)

dataModel.name: journal_entries
dataModel.note: Append-only double-entry ledger. The source of truth. Never updated or deleted. Constraint: per transaction_id, SUM(signed amount) per currency = 0. Index (account_id, entry_id) for statement reads.

fields.name: transaction_id
fields.type: uuid FK -> transactions (groups the balanced entries)

dataModel.name: accounts
dataModel.note: Account metadata so status, ownership, currency policy, and shard assignment are explicit (not just prose). Lets the write path enforce frozen/KYC restrictions and route shard-locally.

dataModel.name: account_balances
dataModel.note: Derived, materialized balance per (account, currency) for fast reads. Durable table, rebuildable from the journal. available = posted - held; withdrawals/transfers check available, not posted.

fields.name: posted_balance
fields.type: bigint (integer minor units; sum of journal entries through watermark)

dataModel.name: holds
dataModel.note: Reservations against available balance (withdrawals, future authorizations). A hold reduces available_balance without a posting; capture posts the debit, release/expire/reverse frees it. Prevents spending the same funds twice while an external rail settles.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Dedup table: a scoped request key + request hash -> its committed result. Key scope is (tenant_id, client_id, account_id, operation, idempotency_key) — a global key would collide across tenants. Reusing a key with a different request_hash returns 409 idempotency_key_reused_with_different_request. Retained at least as long as the client retry + dispute/settlement window; older rows TTL-pruned.

dataModel.name: outbox_events
dataModel.note: Durable outbox written in the posting transaction; a leased relay tails undelivered rows in event_id order, publishes at-least-once, then marks delivered (archived/pruned later). Index (status, event_id) for the undelivered scan.

dataModel.name: external_settlements
dataModel.note: State machine for funding/withdrawals through the external rail. Bridges internal finality and provider settlement. Index (status, expected_by) for the pending-settlement scan.

fields.name: hold_id
fields.type: uuid NULL (withdrawal hold; captured/released on settle)

fields.name: expected_by
fields.type: timestamp (flag if still pending past this)

fields.name: transaction_id
fields.type: uuid NULL (the ledger txn once posted)

dataModel.name: settlement_events
dataModel.note: Ingested provider callbacks. UNIQUE (provider, provider_event_id) makes webhook delivery idempotent so a re-sent settlement callback is processed once.

dataModel.name: cross_shard_transfers
dataModel.note: Coordinator state for transfers whose accounts live on different shards. The durable record the saga is driven from; enables idempotent resume after a coordinator crash.

fields.name: transfer_id
fields.type: uuid PK (also the leg idempotency key)

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(pending_source_posted, pending_destination_posted, posted, compensating, compensated, failed)

patterns.name: Double-entry bookkeeping
patterns.what: Every transaction posts equal debits and credits so the books always balance.
patterns.whenToUse: Any money movement that must be provably conserved and auditable.
patterns.group: Model Money as a Double-Entry Ledger

patterns.name: Immutable append-only ledger
patterns.what: Never mutate or delete a posting; corrections are new compensating entries.
patterns.whenToUse: Auditability and the ability to always reconstruct a balance.
patterns.group: Model Money as a Double-Entry Ledger

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: A client-supplied key the server dedups so a retry doesn't post twice.
patterns.whenToUse: Money-moving APIs over unreliable networks (at-least-once clients).

patterns.name: Materialized balance view
patterns.what: Maintain a derived per-account balance for O(1) reads instead of summing the journal.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy balance access over a large journal.

patterns.name: Optimistic concurrency control
patterns.what: Version the balance row and reject conflicting concurrent updates so two transfers can't lose an update.
patterns.whenToUse: Concurrent balance changes that must not race.

patterns.name: Transactional outbox
patterns.what: Write a downstream event into an outbox table in the same transaction as the postings; a relay tails and publishes it at-least-once.
patterns.whenToUse: Reliably emitting events for a state change without a dual-write that can diverge on a crash.

patterns.name: Clearing account
patterns.what: A per-shard in-transit account that temporarily holds value so each shard-local leg of a cross-shard transfer balances on its own.
patterns.whenToUse: Moving money between accounts on different shards without a distributed transaction.

patterns.name: Saga with compensation
patterns.what: Split a multi-step operation into locally-atomic steps driven by a coordinator that compensates earlier steps if a later one fails.
patterns.whenToUse: Cross-shard or cross-service operations that can't share one transaction but must stay consistent.

patterns.name: Hold / reservation
patterns.what: Reserve funds against available balance without posting, then capture, release, expire, or reverse the hold.
patterns.whenToUse: Withdrawals and authorizations that wait on an asynchronous external rail before becoming final.

patterns.name: Settlement state machine
patterns.what: Model an external movement's lifecycle (pending_external -> settled / failed / reversed) so internal and provider finality stay distinct and reconcilable.
patterns.whenToUse: Funding/withdrawals through a bank or PSP that settle asynchronously.

patterns.name: Reconciliation
patterns.what: Periodically recompute balances from the journal and assert invariants.
patterns.whenToUse: Detecting and correcting any drift between derived state and the source of truth.

steps.title: 1. Naive: A Single Mutable Balance Column (the baseline)
description: Start with the obvious model: each account row has a single balance column. A transfer reads both balances, subtracts from one, adds to the other, and writes them back; a balance read is just SELECT balance.

It's simple and fast, and it's wrong for money in three ways. There is no history — you can explain a balance only by its current number, never by the movements that produced it, so it isn't auditable. Two concurrent transfers can both read the old balance and write back conflicting values, silently losing an update (and possibly overdrawing). And there's nothing to reconcile against: if a bug corrupts the number, nothing can prove or rebuild the right one.
So the baseline motivates the whole design: record every movement as immutable double-entry postings, derive balances from them, and reconcile the derived state against the journal.

view.caption: The client calls the wallet API, which reads and overwrites a single mutable balance column in the account table — no journal, no history yet.

steps.decisionPrompt: A bug leaves an account's balance column wrong. With only a single number stored, how do you even know it's wrong — let alone fix it?
whyNow: The mutable-balance model is what everyone reaches for first; seeing it lose history, race on concurrent writes, and have nothing to reconcile against is precisely what forces an immutable double-entry journal.

recap.after: Each account stores one mutable balance; transfers add/subtract it.
recap.newRisk: No history or audit trail, concurrent transfers lose updates, and a corrupted balance can't be detected or rebuilt.

traps.trap: Storing a single mutable balance and adding/subtracting it.
traps.why: No audit trail, concurrent read-modify-write loses updates, and there's no source of truth to reconcile a corrupted balance against.
traps.instead: Append immutable double-entry postings; derive and reconcile balances from the journal.

steps.title: 2. Model Money as a Double-Entry Ledger
description: Don't store a mutable 'balance' field and add/subtract it — that loses history and is impossible to audit. Instead record every movement as balanced double-entry postings: each transaction writes a debit on one account and an equal credit on another.

The journal is append-only and immutable: the source of truth is the full list of postings, and a balance is the sum of an account's entries. Corrections are new compensating entries, never edits. A transaction header (one row per logical movement) groups its balanced entries and gives them business meaning — type, status, reference, provenance — so any movement is explainable, not just a pair of orphan postings.

Use integer minor units (cents) or fixed-point decimals — never floating point — so amounts are exact. Postings must balance per currency, not just in aggregate: a transaction's debits and credits sum to zero within each currency, so a multi-currency movement (e.g. an FX leg) is modeled as balanced postings per currency, never a single mixed-currency total.

view.caption: The client's transfer goes through the wallet API to the ledger service, which appends balanced double-entry rows transactionally to the ledger store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why record paired debit/credit entries instead of just incrementing a balance column?

concepts.definition: Every transaction posts equal and opposite debits and credits across accounts.
concepts.whyItMatters: The sum of all entries is always zero, so money is provably conserved and every balance is explainable.
concepts.example: Transfer $10: debit a_1 $10, credit a_2 $10 in one transaction.
concepts.group: Model Money as a Double-Entry Ledger

whyNow: The data model is the whole correctness story; leading with an immutable double-entry journal is what a money system requires.

recap.after: An immutable, balanced, auditable journal of all money movement.
recap.newRisk: Summing the journal for every balance read is too slow — need a derived balance and exactness on writes.

traps.trap: Storing a single mutable balance and incrementing it.
traps.why: No history, no audit trail, and concurrent updates silently corrupt it.
traps.instead: Append immutable double-entry postings; derive balances from them.

traps.why: Rounding errors accumulate and the books stop balancing.
traps.instead: Integer minor units or fixed-point decimals.

flows.name: Transfer posts balanced debit + credit (one transaction)
flows.note: A transfer appends paired entries that sum to zero in a single atomic journal transaction. (Deriving a fast balance from this journal is the next step.)

messages.label: append debit a_1 $10 + credit a_2 $10 (1 txn)

steps.title: 3. Idempotent Transfers
description: Networks retry. Without protection, a retried transfer posts twice and moves money twice. The client supplies an idempotency key; the API records key -> result in an idempotency table within the same transaction as the posting.

A repeat of the same key returns the original result without posting again. Also store a hash of the request body and reject a reused key whose payload differs — a key replayed with a different amount or recipient must fail, not silently succeed. This turns at-least-once clients into effectively-once money movement.

The idempotency table lives in the same ledger database/shard as the journal, so the dedup record and the postings commit in one local transaction — a crash can't lose the dedup record while keeping the posting (or vice versa); if they were separate stores, keeping them atomic would require a distributed (2PC) commit. Scope the key to the operation (tenant, client, account, operation) so a key reused across tenants or operations can't collide.

view.caption: Before posting, the wallet API checks the idempotency store for the request, then has the ledger service append entries to the ledger store.

options.name: Idempotency table + request hash, same txn as posting (default)
options.description: A client-supplied idempotency key (plus a hash of the request body) is recorded in the same transaction as the postings, so a retry finds the key and returns the original result without double-posting, while a key reused with a different payload is rejected.
pros: A client-supplied key recorded in the same transaction as the posting makes a retry return the original result without double-posting.
The stored request hash rejects a key replayed with a different amount/recipient instead of silently succeeding.
Works for any operation shape, including transfers with no natural unique business key.
Crash-safe: the dedup record and the postings commit (or roll back) together.
cons: Clients must generate and resend a stable key per logical operation.
The key table grows and needs TTL/cleanup of old keys.
An extra write/lookup on the hot transfer path.

view.caption: The API records the idempotency key in the same transaction the ledger service uses to append entries, so a retried request posts only once.

options.name: Natural-key unique constraint on the journal
options.description: A unique constraint on a stable business key in the journal rejects a duplicate posting at write time, with no separate idempotency store.
pros: No separate idempotency store: a unique constraint on a business key (e.g. external transfer id) rejects the duplicate posting.
The database enforces exactly-once at write time — hard to get wrong.
One fewer component and no key store to expire.
cons: Requires a genuinely unique, client-stable business key — not all transfers have one.
A rejected duplicate surfaces as a constraint error to translate back into the original result.
Couples dedup to the journal schema instead of a general request layer.

view.caption: The ledger service relies on a unique business-key constraint when inserting into the ledger store to reject duplicate postings.

steps.decisionPrompt: A transfer succeeds but the response is lost; the client retries. How do you guarantee the money moves exactly once?

concepts.definition: A client-generated key the server uses to dedup retried mutations.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents double-spending from retries — essential for money APIs.
concepts.example: Second POST with key k_9 returns the first transfer's result, no new postings.

whyNow: Idempotency is the second non-negotiable for money; doing it transactionally with the posting is the correctness nuance.

recap.after: Each logical transfer posts at most once.
recap.newRisk: Concurrent transfers on the same account can still race on the derived balance.

failureDrills.scenario: API crashes after appending the journal but before returning.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Client retries with the same key; the idempotency record (written in the same txn) makes the server return the original result.
failureDrills.mitigation: Write idempotency key + postings in one atomic transaction.

steps.title: 4. Materialized Balances for Fast Reads
description: Summing an account's entire journal on every balance read is too slow once history grows. Maintain a derived account_balances row updated in the same transaction as the postings. This is durable derived state — a table in the ledger database with a watermark and a reconciliation path — not a disposable cache.

Reads hit the materialized balance (O(1)); the journal remains the source of truth, and the balance carries a watermark (last applied entry id) so it can always be recomputed and verified. Because the balance row and the journal append share one local ledger-shard transaction, a read is never out of step with what the journal says (if the two lived in separate stores, keeping them strongly consistent would need 2PC).
Because the balance is derived, it's safe to rebuild from the journal at any time — which is exactly what reconciliation does.

view.caption: The wallet API reads balances from the balance view while the ledger service appends entries to the ledger store and updates that materialized balance.

options.name: Synchronous materialized balance, same txn (default)
options.description: The materialized balance row is updated in the same transaction as the postings, so balance reads are always consistent with the journal and O(1).
pros: The balance updates atomically with the postings, so a read is always consistent with the journal.
O(1) balance reads without summing history.
A watermark (last applied entry id) lets the balance be recomputed and verified against the journal.
cons: Every posting now also writes the balance row — a hot account serializes on it.
Couples write latency to the balance update inside the transaction.
Contention concentrates on the most active accounts' balance rows.

view.caption: The ledger service appends entries and updates the materialized balance view in the same transaction, and the API reads balances from that view.

options.name: Asynchronously updated balance (eventual)
options.description: Postings commit without touching the balance row, and a consumer tailing the journal updates the materialized balance off the hot path.
pros: Postings commit without touching the balance row, so writes are faster and contend less.
A consumer tails the journal and updates balances off the hot path.
Decouples the write throughput from balance maintenance.
cons: Balance is eventually consistent — a read right after a transfer can show a stale number.
Can't safely enforce an overdraft check against a lagging balance.
Adds a separate updater that can itself fall behind or fail, needing reconciliation.

view.caption: The ledger service appends entries to the ledger store, which asynchronously applies postings to the balance view that the API reads.

options.name: Compute balance on read (sum the journal)
options.description: No balance is stored; each read sums the account's journal entries on demand, treating the journal as the single source of truth.
pros: No derived state to keep in sync — the journal is the single source of truth.
Always exactly correct: the balance is the sum of entries by definition.
Simplest write path: just append postings.
cons: Summing an account's entire history per read gets slow as the journal grows.
Hot read paths re-scan large ranges of entries repeatedly.
Overdraft checks become expensive scans on every transfer.

view.caption: The API has the ledger service sum journal entries in the ledger store on each read instead of maintaining a balance view.

steps.decisionPrompt: If balances are derived, why update them transactionally with the journal instead of asynchronously?
whyNow: Fast balance reads are required, and tying the materialized view to the journal transaction keeps it trustworthy rather than eventually-stale.

concepts.definition: A precomputed balance plus the id of the last journal entry it reflects.
concepts.whyItMatters: O(1) reads while staying verifiable against the source of truth.
concepts.example: balance=42.00, last_entry_id=9981 -> recompute from entries > 9981 to verify.

recap.after: O(1) balance reads; still recomputable from the journal.
recap.newRisk: Two concurrent transfers on the same account can both read-modify-write the balance and lose an update.

steps.title: 5. Concurrency Control on Balances
description: Two transfers debiting the same account at once can both read the old balance and write back conflicting values — a lost update, and possibly an overdraft. The journal stays correct, but the derived balance and any balance-check would be wrong.
Guard the balance update with concurrency control: optimistic (version the row; the second writer's compare-and-set fails and retries) or pessimistic (row lock / serialized per-account writes).
Enforce business invariants here too — e.g. reject a transfer that would overdraw, atomically within the same transaction that checks and decrements.

A transfer touches two balance rows (debit and credit), so the transaction updates both under control. With pessimistic locking, always acquire the two account locks in a deterministic order (e.g. by account id) so two opposing transfers can't deadlock; with optimistic CAS, both rows' version checks must pass or the whole transaction retries. Bound that retry: a hot account can livelock on repeated CAS failures, so cap retries with exponential backoff and shed or queue beyond the budget — this is the same hot-account write limit the capacity section calls out. The base design assumes both accounts live in one ledger shard so this stays a single local transaction; when they don't, see the cross-shard sub-step.

view.caption: The wallet API posts through the ledger service, which appends entries to the ledger store and updates the balance view under concurrency control.

options.name: Optimistic concurrency (versioned CAS) (default)
options.description: A writer reads the balance version, computes the new balance, and commits only if the version is unchanged, retrying on conflict with no locks held.
pros: No locks held: read a version, compute, and commit only if the version is unchanged.
Excellent under low contention — most transfers commit first try.
The conditional update enforces check-and-decrement atomically, preventing overdraft.
cons: Under high contention on one account, many writers retry and waste work.
The application must implement retry-on-conflict correctly.
A very hot account can livelock on repeated CAS failures.

view.caption: The ledger service appends entries and updates the versioned balance view with a compare-and-swap, retrying if the version changed.

options.name: Pessimistic row lock per account
options.description: A SELECT ... FOR UPDATE locks the account row for the transaction, serializing all writers so they queue instead of retrying.
pros: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE serializes all writers on the account row — no lost updates by construction.
Predictable under high contention: writers queue instead of retrying.
Simple to reason about — check and decrement under the held lock.
cons: Holding the lock for the transaction's duration limits throughput on hot accounts.
Risk of deadlock when a transfer locks two accounts in inconsistent order.
Lock waits add tail latency under contention.

view.caption: The ledger service takes a SELECT FOR UPDATE row lock per account in the ledger store before appending entries and updating the balance view.

options.name: Serializable isolation (let the DB arbitrate)
options.description: The transaction runs under serializable isolation and the database guarantees a result equal to some serial order, with no hand-written locking or versioning.
pros: The database guarantees the result equals some serial order — strongest correctness with least app logic.
No explicit locking or versioning to hand-write.
Catches subtle anomalies (write skew) that ad-hoc checks can miss.
cons: Serialization failures still force application-level retries.
Highest overhead of the three; throughput drops under contention.
Behavior and cost vary across database engines.

view.caption: The ledger service runs the posting as a serializable transaction over the ledger store, letting the database arbitrate conflicts before updating the balance view.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two $60 withdrawals hit a $100 account simultaneously. How do you prevent both succeeding?

concepts.definition: Read a version, compute, and commit only if the version is unchanged; otherwise retry.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents lost updates without holding locks under low contention.
concepts.example: UPDATE balances SET balance=40, version=8 WHERE id=a_1 AND version=7.

whyNow: Concurrency is where a 'correct on paper' ledger actually stays correct under load; naming lost-update + the overdraft invariant is the senior signal.

recap.before: Concurrent transfers could lose updates / overdraw.
recap.after: Per-account writes serialize; invariants enforced atomically.
recap.newRisk: Hot single account becomes a write bottleneck; cross-account/cross-shard transfers need atomicity.

traps.trap: Checking the balance, then decrementing in a separate step.
traps.why: Two requests both pass the check before either decrements -> overdraft.

bottlenecks.issue: A single very active account serializes all its writes.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Acceptable for correctness; for extreme cases, sub-accounts/buckets that periodically net.
Two-phase commit or an orchestrated saga with compensating entries; keep both postings under one logical transaction.

flows.name: Concurrent debit: compare-and-set on the balance
flows.note: The balance update is a versioned compare-and-set; a concurrent writer's stale version fails and retries, preventing lost updates and overdraft.

steps.title: 5a. Transfers Across Two Shards
description: The base design appends a transfer's debit and credit in one local transaction, which works only when both accounts live in the same ledger shard. Sharding by account id means a transfer between two arbitrary accounts often spans two shards, and there is no single local transaction that can atomically touch both.

Default approach — saga with per-shard clearing accounts driven by a coordinator: a transfer coordinator records the transfer in a durable cross_shard_transfers table (transfer_id, source/destination shard, state, leg ids, retry count, terminal reason) and splits the move into two locally-atomic legs. Leg 1 on shard A debits the source and credits shard A's clearing account in one local transaction; leg 2 on shard B debits shard B's clearing account and credits the destination in another. Each shard owns its own clearing account (a single ledger account belongs to one shard, so there is no account writable on both); the global in-transit position is the sum of the per-shard clearing accounts, which the coordinator and reconciliation audit. The coordinator drives the saga through states pending_source_posted -> pending_destination_posted -> posted, and on a failed second leg posts a compensating credit back to the source on shard A (compensating -> compensated). Every leg is itself balanced double-entry, so the wallet's invariant always holds.

Each leg carries the transfer_id as its idempotency/leg id, so a retried or replayed leg is a no-op — the coordinator can crash and resume from cross_shard_transfers without ever double-posting. Client-visible semantics during the pending window: the source funds are unavailable as soon as leg 1 commits (debited or held), and the destination is not credited until leg 2 posts — surfaced either as 'in transit' or a clearly-marked pending credit, never as spendable on both sides.

Alternative — two-phase commit: a coordinator runs prepare/commit across both shards so the two postings commit together. It is strongly consistent and conceptually simplest, but it holds locks across shards for the duration, blocks on coordinator or participant failure, and ties throughput to the slowest shard — usually the wrong trade for a high-throughput wallet.

Either way, declare it explicitly: the same-shard path is the fast common case; the cross-shard path accepts a brief pending window (saga) or cross-shard locking (2PC) as the cost of spanning shards. Colocating frequently-transacting accounts on one shard keeps most transfers on the fast path.

view.caption: A cross-shard transfer is driven by a coordinator (backed by a durable cross_shard_transfers table) as two locally-atomic legs through per-shard clearing accounts: leg 1 debits the source and credits shard A's clearing; leg 2 debits shard B's clearing and credits the destination; a failed leg 2 triggers a compensating credit on shard A.

steps.decisionPrompt: The debit and credit accounts of a transfer live on different shards. There is no local transaction that spans both — how do you move the money without ever creating or losing a cent?

concepts.definition: A per-shard internal account that temporarily holds value mid-transfer so each shard-local leg balances on its own; the global in-transit position is the sum across shards.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns one cross-shard transfer into two locally-atomic double-entry postings the coordinator can drive, resume, and compensate — no account is writable on both shards.
concepts.example: Shard A: debit a_1, credit A.clearing (pending). Shard B: debit B.clearing, credit a_2 (posted).

whyNow: A wallet transfer naturally touches two accounts; once accounts are sharded, choosing the cross-shard model (saga + clearing account vs 2PC) is the highest-signal staff-level decision in this design.

recap.before: Both accounts assumed colocated in one shard.
recap.after: Cross-shard transfers move via per-shard clearing accounts as two locally-atomic legs, coordinated from a durable transfers table with compensation on failure.
recap.newRisk: A brief pending window where value sits in the per-shard clearing accounts; the coordinator must drive every saga to a terminal state and resume idempotently after a crash.

traps.trap: Assuming a single SQL transaction can atomically debit and credit accounts on different shards.
traps.why: A local transaction can't span shards; the second write can fail after the first commits, leaving money created or destroyed.
traps.instead: Split into locally-atomic legs through per-shard clearing accounts (saga + compensation) or pay for cross-shard 2PC.

traps.trap: Using one shared clearing account that both shards write to.
traps.why: A ledger account belongs to a single shard; writing it from another shard re-introduces the cross-shard write you were trying to avoid.
traps.instead: Give each shard its own clearing account and audit the global in-transit position as their sum.

failureDrills.scenario: Leg 1 (source debit + clearing credit on shard A) commits, then the coordinator crashes before posting leg 2.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: On restart the coordinator reads cross_shard_transfers, finds the transfer in pending_source_posted, and idempotently resumes — re-posting leg 2 (a no-op if it already landed via the transfer_id leg key) or compensating leg 1 if leg 2 is unrecoverable. Money never sits lost: it is in shard A's clearing account until a terminal state is reached.
failureDrills.mitigation: Drive every saga from the durable coordinator table; make each leg idempotent on transfer_id; reconcile clearing-account balances to flag any transfer stuck pending past its window.

steps.title: 6. Reliable Downstream Events (Outbox)
description: Other systems (notifications, statements, fraud, analytics) need to know about each posting. Don't write the event in a separate step — a crash between the ledger write and the event publish would diverge them.

Use the transactional outbox: write the event into an outbox table in the same ledger-database transaction as the postings (the outbox is a durable table colocated with the journal, not a separate queue on the write path). The event row exists if and only if the posting committed. A separate relay process then tails outbox rows and streams them to the broker at-least-once, marking each delivered.

Consumers dedup on transaction id (the events are naturally idempotent because the ledger is), so downstream stays consistent with the source of truth. Operate the relay deliberately: monitor relay lag, retry failed publishes, dedup on an event id, and prune delivered rows so the table doesn't grow unbounded.

view.caption: The ledger service appends entries and writes an event to the outbox in the same transaction for reliable downstream delivery.

options.name: Transactional outbox (default)
options.description: The downstream event row commits in the same transaction as the postings, and a relay streams outbox rows to the broker at least once.
pros: The event row commits in the same transaction as the postings — it exists iff the posting committed.
No dual-write divergence: a crash can't leave a posting without its event or vice versa.
A relay streams outbox rows at-least-once; consumers dedup on transaction id (the ledger is naturally idempotent).
cons: Requires an outbox table and a relay/poller to tail and publish it.
Adds a small write to every transaction and some publish latency.
Outbox rows need pruning after delivery.

view.caption: The ledger service writes the ledger entries and the outbox event in one transaction so the event can't be lost.

options.name: Publish directly to Kafka after commit
options.description: The service commits the posting and then publishes the event to the broker as a separate step, with no outbox row in the transaction.
pros: Simplest to wire up: commit the posting, then call the broker.
No outbox table or relay to operate.
Lowest steady-state latency when nothing fails.
cons: A crash between commit and publish loses the event — posting exists, downstream never hears it.
A failed publish after a committed posting is a dual-write divergence with no clean recovery.
Unacceptable for money, where every posting must produce exactly one event.

view.caption: The ledger service appends entries and then publishes to Kafka after commit, which may drop the event if publishing fails.

options.name: Change data capture (tail the DB log)
options.description: A CDC connector tails the database's commit log and derives downstream events from the committed postings, with no extra in-transaction write.
pros: Reuses the committed postings directly — events are derived from the database's own log, never a separate write.
No outbox table or extra in-transaction write on the hot path.
Guaranteed to reflect exactly what committed, in commit order.
cons: Couples downstream event shape to the physical table schema, which is brittle to migrations.
Requires running and operating a CDC connector (e.g. log decoding).
Harder to emit a clean domain event than an explicitly shaped outbox row.

view.caption: The ledger service only writes to the ledger store, and a CDC tailer reads the commit log to emit downstream events.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why not just publish a Kafka event right after committing the ledger write?

concepts.definition: Write domain events into a table within the business transaction; relay them afterward.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees the event exists iff the posting committed — no dual-write divergence.
concepts.example: Posting + outbox row commit together; a relay tails the outbox to the stream.

whyNow: Money systems must never have an event without a posting or vice versa; the outbox is the standard guarantee and reuses the ledger's atomicity.

recap.before: Events published separately could diverge from the ledger.
recap.after: Events are emitted atomically with postings, delivered at-least-once.
recap.newRisk: Derived state (balances, exports) can still drift due to bugs — need a backstop check.

steps.title: 7. Reconciliation, Audit, and Settlement
description: Reconcile at two cadences. A continuous, cheap invariant check (system-wide debits == credits, and per-account materialized balance == sum of journal entries through its watermark — entries after the watermark are unapplied tail work, not drift). Plus a scheduled full reconciliation that recomputes every balance from the journal and matches external provider reports. Any mismatch raises an alert with a defined threshold; the journal is authoritative, so repair means rebuilding derived state from it or posting a compensating entry — never editing history. Keep a documented replay/rebuild procedure: a balance table can always be regenerated by replaying the journal.

Disaster recovery has a precise replay boundary: pick a recovery checkpoint of {journal commit position, balance watermark (last_entry_id), outbox event id, settlement report watermark}. After a partial restore, region failover, or relay replay, rebuild each balance from journal entries through the checkpoint and then tail forward; re-drive outbox from its last delivered event id (consumers dedup on transaction id); resume the settlement tracker from the last matched provider report. Idempotency records must be retained at least as long as the client retry window and restored consistently with the committed transactions they dedup, or a post-restore retry could double-post. The invariant after restore is: balance equals the sum through its watermark; anything past the watermark is tail work to replay, not an error.
Export immutable statements/audit records for compliance; the append-only journal makes any historical balance reconstructable on demand.

Model external movements as a state machine, not just a reconciliation correction. A top-up or withdrawal opens as pending_external; a withdrawal places a hold against available_balance. The rail settles asynchronously and can confirm (-> settled: capture the hold and post the entries), fail (-> failed: release the hold), or reverse a prior settlement (-> reversed: post a compensating entry). Internal ledger finality and external settlement finality are different things, and the settlement tracker is what bridges them. Scheduled reconciliation matches the tracker against the provider's settlement report and flags any movement stuck pending past its expected window.

view.caption: The reconciliation worker scans the journal, verifies and rebuilds the balance table, exports statements to the audit store, and matches the settlement tracker against provider reports; the external rail calls back on settlement so the tracker can post or compensate.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you detect that a bug caused a materialized balance to drift from the journal?

concepts.definition: Recompute derived state from the source of truth and assert invariants hold.
concepts.whyItMatters: Catches drift and corruption early; the journal is always the authority for correction.
concepts.example: Nightly: sum(entries) per account == account_balances; global debits == credits.

whyNow: Closing on reconciliation + audit + external settlement completes the correctness story: even bugs and external mismatches are detectable and fixable.

recap.before: Derived state trusted blindly; external rails unverified.
recap.after: Balances continuously verified; statements auditable; external settlement reconciled.
recap.newRisk: Reconciliation lag and external settlement delays — bounded by invariant checks and compensating entries.

failureDrills.scenario: An external withdrawal is reported failed after the wallet already debited.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reconciliation flags the mismatch; a compensating credit reverses the debit.
failureDrills.mitigation: Match internal postings to settlement reports; post compensating entries, never edit history.

failureDrills.scenario: Restore from a backup where outbox delivery is ahead of the balance watermark (more events were published than the restored balances reflect).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Rebuild balances from the journal through the checkpoint and tail forward; re-drive outbox from its last delivered event id. Re-published events are deduped downstream by event/transaction id, so no double effects — the journal, not the restored derived state, is authoritative.
failureDrills.mitigation: Restore to a consistent checkpoint {journal position, balance watermark, outbox event id, settlement watermark}; replay by journal position; dedup downstream on event/transaction id.

flows.name: External withdrawal: pending -> settle or compensate
flows.note: A withdrawal opens as pending_external and holds funds; the provider callback either settles (post the external leg) or fails (compensating credit reverses the hold). Internal finality lags external settlement.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Double-Entry Wallet Ledger
finalDesign.description: The wallet API enforces idempotency (scoped key + request hash -> result) and posts every transfer as balanced double-entry entries into an immutable journal — the source of truth. The journal, idempotency table, materialized balance table, holds, and outbox table all live in the same ledger database/shard, so a single local transaction appends the postings, updates the per-account balance (guarded by optimistic concurrency and overdraft invariants against available_balance), records the idempotency result, and writes the outbox row together — no distributed commit on the hot path. Balance reads hit the materialized table; a leased relay streams outbox events at-least-once; a reconciliation worker recomputes balances from the journal, asserts debits==credits, exports immutable statements, and matches external-rail settlements via a settlement tracker, correcting drift only via compensating entries. Synchronous vs asynchronous. Synchronous — invariants that hold inside the posting transaction: journal entries balance per currency, the idempotency result commits, the balance row updates, the outbox row exists, and the overdraft invariant holds against available balance. Asynchronous — eventual workflows: outbox relay delivery, reconciliation scans, statement export, provider settlement, and cross-shard saga completion. Scope: this single-transaction path assumes both accounts in a transfer share one ledger shard; a transfer whose debit and credit accounts live on different shards uses the cross-shard saga (per-shard clearing accounts + a coordinator driven from cross_shard_transfers, with compensation on a failed leg). External top-ups and withdrawals are not internally final until the rail settles — a withdrawal places a hold against available_balance and they move through pending_external / settled / failed / reversed states, with a compensating entry on failure or reversal. After a disaster, derived state is rebuilt to a consistent checkpoint {journal position, balance watermark, outbox event id, settlement watermark} and replayed forward.

view.caption: The client's transfers go through the wallet API (idempotency-checked) to the ledger service, which in one ledger-shard transaction appends double-entry rows, updates the balance table, and writes an outbox row; a relay streams outbox events, a reconciliation worker verifies balances and exports audits, external funding/withdrawals move through a settlement tracker reconciled against the rail, and cross-shard transfers are driven by a coordinator through per-shard clearing accounts on shards A and B.

functional.how: Materialized balance derived from the journal.
Balanced debit/credit postings in one transaction (same shard); cross-shard via per-shard clearing accounts + a coordinator saga with compensation.

functional.requirement: Top up / withdraw via external rail
functional.how: External funding/withdrawal as a pending_external -> settled/failed/reversed state machine; withdrawals hold funds against available_balance until the rail settles, reconciled to postings.

nonFunctional.how: Idempotency key + request-hash dedup, transactional with the posting.
Per-account serialized writes with concurrency control; deterministic lock ordering across the two accounts.

say: Establish correctness over availability — this is a money system.
Pin idempotency, strong consistency, and auditability as hard requirements.
Agree balances are read-heavy but writes must be exact.
Model money as an immutable double-entry journal (source of truth).
Add idempotency keys for safe retries.
Materialize balances for fast reads, updated transactionally.
Concurrency control on balances; check-and-decrement atomically.
Reconciliation, audit, and external settlement matching.
Map requirements to mechanisms; state that the write-path tables (journal, idempotency, balance, outbox) share one ledger-shard transaction.

Tradeoffs: per-account write serialization, cross-shard transfers via a clearing account + compensation (or 2PC), reconciliation lag, and external settlement that is final only when the rail confirms.
Mention multi-currency, holds/authorizations, and the operational alerts as extensions.

expectations: Uses a ledger and avoids mutable balances.
May miss concurrency control and reconciliation.
Chooses a concrete cross-shard model — saga with an in-transit clearing account and compensation (default) vs cross-shard 2PC — and states the pending-window trade-off.
Designs reconciliation cadence and the external-settlement state machine (pending_external / settled / failed / reversed).
Names the operational signals (idempotency conflicts, CAS retries, lock waits, outbox lag, reconciliation drift, settlement mismatches, hot-account throttling) and discusses multi-currency precisely.

followUps: How would you support a transfer across two shards atomically?
How do you handle multi-currency wallets and FX without rounding errors?
How would you implement holds/authorizations (reserve then capture)?
How do you scale a single very hot account's writes?
How do you correct a posting made in error without violating immutability?
Which operational signals would you alert on (idempotency conflicts, CAS retries, lock waits, outbox lag, reconciliation drift, settlement mismatches, hot-account throttling)?
How do you recover after a disaster so the journal, idempotency records, and replay ordering stay mutually consistent?
